text: Four 8.5mm ports are placed in a straight line to access the left lower quadrant and pelvis. The surgeon begins the procedure by lysing adhesions and removing attachments around the colostomy in the pelvic region.

The surgeon prepares the pelvic area for anastomosis by identifying previously placed sutures and the peritoneal reflection. Using a bipolar energy device, the surgeon carefully dissects and divides attachments, scar tissue, and inflamed tissue, ensuring preservation of blood supply while dividing the colon distally and proximally with short bursts.

The surgeon divides the descending colon distally to prepare for an end-to-end anastomosis, ensuring there is no tension. A 3-0 barbed suture is used in a running fashion, starting from the back wall. The view switches to ICG mode to assess blood supply during the anastomosis, with intravenous ICG administered in two cc doses. The Cornell suture technique is employed.

The surgeon uses an ICG view to continuously assess the blood supply during the anastomosis, ensuring no gaps are present as the sutures meet in the middle anteriorly. The sutures are then locked on both sides.

The surgeon tests the anastomosis by filling the pelvis with water and performing a sigmoidoscopy, confirming no leaks. The remnant of the distal end is placed in an endobag and extracted through the lateral port. The stoma is visible, and the colostomy area is excised and the fascia closed appropriately.
